planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mexican fan palm is extremely fast-growing — potentially reaching 80–100 feet over several decades — and is one of the defining large palms of warm-climate urban landscapes from the Gulf Coast to California. In zone 9 it is a reliable, self-maintaining landscape palm. Unlike W. filifera (California fan palm), it has a notably more slender trunk and faster growth rate. The dead fronds persist as a 'skirt' below the crown unless removed. Hardy to about 20°F when established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mexican fan palm is the tall, slender-trunked, fast-growing signature palm of warm-climate cities from Houston and New Orleans to Los Angeles and Miami — a native of the oases and canyon streams of Baja California and Sonora, Mexico that has been introduced throughout the subtropical and warm-temperate world as one of the most widely planted of all landscape palms. The palm grows with remarkable speed — adding several feet of trunk per year under good conditions — and eventually reaches 80–100 feet or more, developing an extremely slender, slightly tapering, gray-brown trunk (notably more slender than the related California fan palm W. filifera) topped with a compact, dense crown of large, circular, fan-shaped leaves on long, slightly spiny petioles. The dead fronds remain attached below the living crown, forming the characteristic drooping 'skirt' or 'petticoat' of persistent dead fronds that is a recognizable feature of Washingtonia palms and that harbors wildlife habitat (and occasional fire risk — many municipalities require removal). Mexican fan palm is one of the defining skyline elements of warm-climate cities — a tall, slender, vertical accent of pure tropical character growing rapidly to heights that create the subtropical urban landscape visual. Drought-tolerant once established; requires minimal care in suitable climates.

subcategory: native American fan palm with filamentous leaves

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; California fan palm is the only palm native to western North America — growing wild at desert oases in the Colorado Desert of California and Baja California where underground water reaches the surface. More cold-hardy than W. robusta (to about 15°F) and substantially stockier in trunk, growing more slowly to a large, massively trunked specimen. The leaf segments have characteristic thread-like white filaments along their margins. A defining plant of California desert oasis landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; California fan palm is the only palm species native to the wild landscapes of western North America — a native of the oasis springs and seeps of the Colorado Desert of California and adjacent Baja California, where it forms the iconic oasis groves in which the combination of towering, massively trunked palms and the lush green contrast of palm fronds against the arid desert landscape represents one of the most dramatic plant-landscape compositions in North American horticulture. The trunk of mature California fan palms is massively, impressively stocky — much wider in diameter than the slender W. robusta — developing over decades and centuries into a thick-waisted, gray-tan column of considerable girth that supports the characteristic large, circular, gray-green fan leaves. The leaf segments carry distinctive thin, white, thread-like filaments along the margins — the 'filifera' (thread-bearing) of the species name — that shimmer in breeze and sun. The full-sized crown of large, wavy-ribbed, circular fan leaves surrounded by the characteristic hanging skirt of persistent dead fronds creates the quintessential California desert-oasis palm silhouette. More cold-hardy than W. robusta (surviving to approximately 15°F) and longer-lived, developing over centuries into ancient, massive specimens of extraordinary character in suitable landscapes. The defining palm of California and the warm desert Southwest.

subcategory: most cold-hardy banana, rapid tropical effect perennial

planting.note: Plant divisions (rhizome offsets) or container plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile soil in full sun to part shade after frost danger has passed; hardy banana is the most cold-hardy banana species, dying back to the ground in frost but regrowng vigorously from the underground rhizome in spring as far north as zone 5 with heavy mulch over winter. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a fully evergreen or briefly dormant perennial, reaching 10–14 feet in a single growing season. It does not produce edible fruit (fruits are small, pithy, and full of seeds) but is grown for its spectacular tropical foliage.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile soil in full sun to part shade after frost danger has passed; hardy banana is the single most cold-hardy banana species in cultivation and the essential 'tropical effect' structural foliage plant for gardeners in temperate climates who want the bold, lush, unmistakably tropical visual impact of banana plants without a truly tropical climate. It is native to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan and adjacent coastal China, where it experiences cold winters — surviving ground temperatures that kill the aerial stems (the pseudostems) by maintaining the underground rhizome and crown intact, from which it regrows vigorously the following spring. In USDA zones 5–7 it dies completely to the ground each winter and regrows from the rhizome each spring; with heavy mulch (12–18 inches of straw or leaves) over the crown in late fall, the rhizome survives reliably to zone 5. In zone 8–9 the pseudostems (the apparent 'trunks', actually composed of tightly rolled leaf bases) often survive the winter partially and grow more rapidly in spring by retaining their height. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is effectively a permanent perennial, growing each season to 10–14 feet with massive, paddle-shaped, lush green leaves 3–6 feet long that provide tropical effect unmatched by any other reliably cold-hardy plant. The pseudostem clumps increase in size annually as the rhizome produces offsets. In late summer of mature clumps an inflorescence may emerge and produce the typical banana flower and small, pithy, seed-filled fruits — ornamental but not culinary. Provides the fastest large-scale tropical structural effect in the temperate and subtropical garden.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; pink velvet banana is a compact ornamental species from Assam and northeastern India, growing to 4–6 feet and producing distinctive, vivid pink, velvet-textured, self-peeling fruits about 3 inches long. The fruits are ornamental and technically edible but filled with hard seeds and are not grown for culinary use. More cold-hardy than most ornamental bananas — surviving to zone 8 with some protection. Outstanding as a seasonal or permanent tropical ornamental in zone 8–11 gardens.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; pink velvet banana is a compact, ornamental banana from the hill forests and moist slopes of northeastern India (Assam) and Bangladesh that provides two distinct ornamental seasons in the garden: a summer season of lush, tropical, paddle-shaped banana foliage in a compact (4–6 foot) plant suitable for small gardens and containers, and a late-summer to autumn fruiting season producing clusters of the remarkable, distinctive, vivid pink, velvet-textured fruits that give the species its common names. The fruits are small — about 3 inches long — and covered in a dense, soft, velvety pink to hot-pink skin of bright, saturated pink-magenta color that is completely unlike the yellow-green of edible bananas. They are technically edible but filled with numerous very hard, black seeds and are gathered primarily for their ornamental value — the clusters of vivid pink velvet fruits against the tropical green foliage are a striking visual combination. The name 'self-peeling' banana refers to the fruit's tendency to split and peel back naturally when ripe, revealing the cream-white interior. More cold-hardy than many ornamental bananas, this species tolerates zone 8 winters with mulch protection over the rhizome and regrows reliably in spring. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a permanent, clumping perennial that provides compact tropical foliage and the unique pink-velvet ornamental fruit display annually.

subcategory: shade-tolerant clustering palm with bipinnate fishtail leaves

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; clustering fishtail palm is unique among commonly grown palms in having bipinnate (twice-divided) leaves — the individual leaflets are further divided into irregular, jagged, triangular to wedge-shaped segments with ragged tips that resemble fish tails. The clustering habit (multiple stems per clump) means individual stems can die after flowering without killing the whole plant. In zone 9 it requires a protected, warm microclimate; in zone 10-11 it thrives. Shade tolerant.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; clustering fishtail palm is botanically unique among the commonly grown palms in producing bipinnate leaves — leaves that are divided not once (as in all other feather palms, where a central rachis carries rows of leaflets) but twice, the primary leaflets themselves being further divided into multiple secondary segments. These secondary segments are irregular, asymmetrical, wedge-shaped to triangular, with a jagged, torn, irregular margin at the wide outer tip that closely resembles the tail of a fish — hence the common name 'fishtail palm'. The overall textural effect of the bipinnate, fishtail-segmented leaf is unlike any other palm — a complex, slightly disheveled, fern-like yet bold tropical texture that catches light differently from all directions and creates an immediately distinctive, unusual palm silhouette. The clustering habit (the palm grows as a clump of multiple individual stems) is equally important: because individual caryota stems are monocarpic (they flower from top to bottom over several years and then die), the clustering species maintains the clump indefinitely as new stems continuously replace old ones — unlike monocarpic single-trunk species. More shade-tolerant than most large palms. The sap and fruits of Caryota species contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic to skin and mucous membranes — avoid contact with fruit pulp.
